paper_title: The Relationship Between Workload and Non-Contact Musculoskeletal Injury in College Swimmers: A Prospective Cohort Study

methods.description: Process for calculating various types of workloads for participants based on practice attendance and training logs to assess their impact on injury risk.

steps.description: Calculate weekly workloads (Monday through Sunday) for each participant based on practice attendance and team training/competition logs.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate weekly workloads from practice logs

steps.description: Categorize workload in four ways: overall workload, acute workload, chronic workload, and ACWR.
steps.searchable_summary: categorize workloads into overall, acute, chronic, and ACWR

steps.description: Calculate overall workload as the number of kilometers swum over the entire season.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate overall workload as total kilometers swum

steps.description: Calculate acute workload for each week as the number of kilometers swum that week.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate acute workload as weekly kilometers swum

steps.description: Calculate chronic workload for each week as the average kilometers swum per week over that week and the preceding three weeks.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate chronic workload as average over four weeks

steps.description: Calculate ACWR for each week as the quotient of the acute workload and chronic workload.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate ACWR as acute workload divided by chronic workload

steps.description: During the first three weeks of the season, factor self-reported preseason workloads into chronic workload and ACWR calculations.
steps.searchable_summary: include preseason workloads in early season calculations

steps.description: Calculate all workloads in Microsoft Excel (Version 16, Microsoft Corp., Redmond, WA, USA).

methods.description: Statistical procedures used to analyze the collected data to determine the relationship between workload and injury risk.

steps.description: Use the Shapiro-Wilk Test and visual inspection of histograms to determine normality of all data.
steps.searchable_summary: test data normality using Shapiro-Wilk and histograms

steps.description: Describe normally distributed data with mean ± standard deviation; describe non-normally distributed data with median (interquartile range) (IQR).
steps.searchable_summary: summarize data with mean/SD or median/IQR

steps.description: Use independent samples t-tests to assess differences in demographic data between injured and uninjured student-athletes, except for age and months per year swum (Mann-Whitney U tests) and sex and training groups (Fisher's exact test).
steps.searchable_summary: perform t-tests, Mann-Whitney U tests, and Fisher's exact test on demographic data

steps.description: Use logistic regression to assess the association between the overall workload throughout the season and injury.
steps.searchable_summary: perform logistic regression on workload and injury

steps.description: Pool data for acute workload, chronic workload, and ACWR for all athlete-weeks of training and dichotomize each into 'high' and 'typical'.
steps.searchable_summary: dichotomize workload data into high and typical

steps.description: Calculate three logistic regression models using generalized estimating equations (GEE) to assess the association between workload and injury.
steps.searchable_summary: use GEE for logistic regression on workload and injury

steps.description: Calculate injury rate for a given range of acute workloads and ACWRs.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate injury rate for workload ranges

steps.description: Complete all statistical analyses in SPSS (Version 24, IBM Corp., Armonk, NY).
